title: Creating a dashboard of status by length of time in each status in days
excerpt: Ultimately trying to see how we can work on our efficiencies by lowering the timeline, but need to see what our current numbers are to get a baseline and understanding of the gaps to solve for.

posts.author: kzwettler (Kristina)
posts.cookedHtml:
Ultimately trying to see how we can work on our efficiencies by lowering the timeline, but need to see what our current numbers are to get a baseline and understanding of the gaps to solve for.

Screenshot 2026-02-10 135756831×337 30.3 KB

posts.author: drtanvisachar (Dr. Tanvi Sachar- Certified Monday.com Partner)
posts.cookedHtml:
Hello @kzwettler
Totally agree with this approach. Before shortening timelines, it helps to get a baseline on how long work sits in each status and where items get stuck.

Best quick options:
Use a Dashboard to track cycle time and workload
Add Time Tracking to measure time per phase
Use Board Insights to spot bottlenecks by status

If you’d like hands-on help or want us to walk through this live, you can book a 1:1 paid 60-minute strategy session with our team here:
Calendly

posts.author: Clover_Gal (Desiree McCarthy | monday.com Ambassador)
posts.cookedHtml:
You’ll need to add columns for each status ie a ‘time tracking’ column for Building. Then when status changes to Building, set an automation to start that timer. Then when status changes from Building to something else, stop that timer. Or you could do similarly with date columns ie when status changes to Building, set ‘Building date column’ to ‘today’.

Desiree - www.thecleverclovers.com

posts.author: Mariia (Mariia Mykhasiuk)
posts.cookedHtml:
hi @kzwettler ! If you’re trying to establish a clear baseline before improving timelines, you might want to look at Time in Status for monday.com. It tracks how long items stay in each status and gives you real data on bottlenecks and delays, so you can see exactly where time is being lost and where efficiency improvements will have the biggest impact.

There’s a 14-day trial, and happy to walk through the setup on a quick demo if helpful.

posts.author: alex_m_89 (Alex Miller)
posts.cookedHtml: the date column approach @Clover_Gal mentioned is solid for this. we did something similar, added a “days in status” formula column that calculates the difference between today and the date stamp. then you can group by status on a dashboard and see average days per stage pretty easily. way simpler than time tracking columns imo.

posts.author: sl1nna (Sami Linnanvuo)
posts.cookedHtml:
@kzwettler if an add-on is an option, I suggest taking a look at the Screenful app, which provides analytics and reporting of monday.com data. You can customize reports with advanced filters and schedule them to be sent via email or Slack.

Screenful calculates timings for each item automatically and shows the average timings for each status. You can learn more from this post: Track time in status for monday.com items

posts.author: nitesh (Nitesh Singh)
posts.cookedHtml:
Hi @kzwettler ,

Hi Kristina — building on Alex’s approach above, here is how to get it into a dashboard so you can see averages across all items at once rather than row by row.

Once you have a date column per status (e.g. “Entered Building Date”) and a formula column calculating days in that status using DAYS({Today}, {Entered Building Date}) — add a Numbers widget to your dashboard, set it to show the average of that formula column filtered to items currently in that status. Repeat one widget per status stage.

This gives you a single dashboard view showing “Average days in Prospecting: 4.2 / Average days in Building: 11.6 / Average days in Review: 3.1” — exactly the baseline you described needing before you can identify where the bottleneck is.

One practical tip: the formula column will keep counting even after an item moves to the next status unless you clear the date. Add an automation for each transition — “When status changes from Building to Review, clear Entered Building Date” — so the counter resets cleanly and your historical average stays accurate.
